refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit reference to measurement/monitoring (telemetry) and duty cycle context to make verification concrete. High safety criticality tailoring requires that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle; p95 cycle time computed from telemetry logs; comparison against CT_target acceptance threshold; safety event correlation analysis to ensure no safety compromise.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The teleoperation system shall introduce latency and control overhead not exceeding allocated cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below target, except during safety-critical interventions.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report cycle time metrics via telemetry for performance tracking.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Optimize vehicle hydraulic/mechanical actuation timing; minimize travel path via route planning; parallelize load/dump operations where safe.
DP-B: Implement predictive cycle time modeling with real-time adjustment of operator commands and vehicle parameters based on duty cycle state.
DP-C: Establish tiered teleoperation latency budget (e.g., ≤200ms round-trip) and enforce network QoS; use local autonomy for time-critical sub-cycles.
DP-D: Design safety services as non-blocking asynchronous monitors with safety-critical decisions pre-computed; avoid synchronous safety checks in hot-path cycle execution.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-Integrated: Combine DP-A (vehicle mechanical optimization), DP-C (teleoperation latency budget with local autonomy), and DP-D (non-blocking safety monitoring). This multi-faceted approach addresses cycle time at the vehicle level, network/control level, and safety level without creating single points of failure or safety compromise.

potential_sources: Vehicle actuation timing (DP-A) must be coordinated with teleoperation command latency (DP-C) to avoid operator over-correction or under-utilization.
Safety monitoring (DP-D) must not introduce hidden synchronous waits in vehicle control loops; requires careful interface design.
Route planning and load prediction (DP-B concept) could enhance DP-A but is deferred to sub-level decomposition.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable via explicit latency budgets, asynchronous safety architecture, and clear ownership boundaries between vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. No fundamental contradiction detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: CT_target is achievable within vehicle mechanical limits and network latency constraints; risk: if target is unrealistic, no DP can satisfy it.
Assumption: Local autonomy (DP-C) can be safely implemented for time-critical sub-cycles; risk: requires robust fallback and safety validation.
Assumption: Non-blocking safety monitoring (DP-D) can detect and respond to hazards within acceptable latency; risk: if safety response time is too long, cycle time optimization may be unsafe.
Risk: Teleoperation latency variability (network jitter) may cause p95 to exceed p50 significantly; mitigation: design for worst-case latency, not average.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify latency budget allocation: e.g., 'Teleoperation system shall maintain ≤150ms round-trip latency for 95th percentile under site network conditions; vehicle shall execute sub-cycles with ≤50ms actuation response time.' This makes the DP testable and allocates responsibility clearly.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level integration module that orchestrates cycle time performance across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. It owns the cycle time KPI measurement, reporting via Telemetry, and coordination of latency budgets across sub-systems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits cycle time metrics (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end timestamps; computed p95 cycle time) to external monitoring/analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives operator/site manager tasking (load location, dump location, cycle priority) that influences cycle time optimization strategy.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety alerts from Safety Services and may issue cycle time hold/resume commands in response to safety events.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution manages network connectivity and latency monitoring for Teleoperation sub-system; reports network health to Telemetry.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of cycle time KPI. It integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; owns measurement, reporting, and cross-system coordination. Minimal diffusion: only latency budget enforcement and telemetry aggregation are contained here.

Vehicle owns mechanical/hydraulic actuation timing (DP-A) and local sub-cycle execution. However, cycle time KPI is system-level; Vehicle cannot own the full requirement because it does not control teleoperation latency or safety service delays. Diffusion: Vehicle must expose cycle time sub-metrics (load time, travel time, dump time) to NGHE Solution.

Teleoperation owns latency budget enforcement (DP-C) and local autonomy for time-critical sub-cycles. However, it does not own vehicle actuation or safety monitoring. Diffusion: Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle on command timing and with Safety Services on safety-critical holds.

Safety Services owns non-blocking safety monitoring (DP-D) and must ensure safety checks do not block cycle execution. However, Safety Services does not own cycle time optimization; it is a constraint provider. Diffusion: Safety Services must expose safety status and latency impact to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because: (1) Cycle time KPI is a system-level performance metric that requires integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) NGHE Solution owns the measurement, aggregation, and reporting of cycle time via Telemetry; (3) NGHE Solution can enforce latency budgets and coordinate trade-offs between sub-systems without duplicating logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are sub-modules that contribute to cycle time but do not own the KPI.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a thin orchestration layer without clear ownership of measurement logic, cycle time metrics may be computed inconsistently across sub-systems. Mitigation: Define cycle time measurement algorithm (timestamps, filtering, p95 computation) as a concrete artifact owned by NGHE Solution.

Risk: Latency budget enforcement may require real-time coordination between Teleoperation and Vehicle; if NGHE Solution is not responsive, budgets may be violated. Mitigation: Implement latency monitoring as a continuous background task in NGHE Solution with alerts to Teleoperation and Vehicle.

Risk: Safety Services may introduce unpredictable delays if safety checks are not truly non-blocking. Mitigation: Require Safety Services to expose worst-case latency impact and validate that it is within cycle time budget.
Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into latency variability (jitter). Mitigation: Enhance Network Link to report latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) not just average.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define NGHE Solution's cycle time measurement module as a concrete artifact: 'NGHE Solution shall contain a Cycle Time Monitor (IM-CTM) that: (1) receives sub-cycle timestamps from Vehicle via Telemetry; (2) computes p95 cycle time over a rolling window; (3) reports metrics and alerts to external systems via Telemetry interface; (4) enforces latency budgets by monitoring Network Link and signaling Teleoperation if latency exceeds threshold.' This makes the IM boundary concrete and testable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (integrated approach combining vehicle optimization, teleoperation latency budget, and non-blocking safety monitoring) directly addresses the FR requirement to achieve p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target. The DP is logically sound and does not introduce contradictions. Coupling between DP elements is manageable via explicit latency budgets and asynchronous safety architecture.

diffusion_risks: Cycle time measurement logic is distributed across Vehicle (sub-cycle timing), Teleoperation (latency monitoring), and Safety Services (safety delay impact). NGHE Solution must aggregate these signals; risk of inconsistent measurement if sub-modules do not expose metrics in a standardized format. Mitigation: Define a Cycle Time Metrics Interface (part of Telemetry) with standardized timestamp and latency fields.

Latency budget enforcement requires coordination between NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, and Vehicle. If enforcement logic is duplicated across modules, budgets may be violated inconsistently. Mitigation: Centralize budget enforcement in NGHE Solution; Teleoperation and Vehicle query budget status via a shared interface.

Safety Services non-blocking design is critical to DP success. If Safety Services introduces hidden synchronous waits, cycle time will degrade. Mitigation: Require Safety Services to expose worst-case latency impact and validate via testing.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for cycle time KPI ownership. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are sub-modules that contribute to cycle time but do not own the KPI. Clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) enable coordination without diffusion. Recommended: Define a Cycle Time Metrics sub-interface within Telemetry to standardize measurement across sub-modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time KPI can be verified via instrumented field trials: (1) Deploy Cycle Time Monitor in NGHE Solution; (2) Collect sub-cycle timestamps from Vehicle, latency data from Teleoperation, and safety event logs from Safety Services; (3) Compute p95 cycle time from telemetry logs; (4) Compare against CT_target. Verification is feasible if sub-modules expose required metrics via Telemetry interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns cycle time KPI measurement and reporting; Vehicle owns sub-cycle execution timing; Teleoperation owns latency budget enforcement; Safety Services owns non-blocking safety monitoring. Each module has a concrete responsibility. Recommended: Document allocation in a Responsibility Matrix (RACI) to ensure stakeholders understand ownership.

recommended_revisions: Refine Telemetry interface to include a Cycle Time Metrics sub-interface with standardized fields: load_start_ts, travel_start_ts, dump_start_ts, dump_end_ts, sub_cycle_latencies, safety_delay_impact. This ensures consistent measurement across sub-modules.

Define a Latency Budget Interface (part of Network Link) that allows NGHE Solution to query and enforce latency budgets in real-time. Teleoperation and Vehicle shall query this interface before executing time-critical operations.

Require Safety Services to expose a Safety Latency Impact Interface that reports worst-case delay introduced by safety checks. NGHE Solution shall validate that safety latency is within cycle time budget.

Add a Cycle Time Optimization Strategy Interface (part of Tasking) that allows NGHE Solution to adjust optimization parameters (e.g., travel speed, load prediction) based on duty cycle state and CT_target.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and the selected DP is logically sound. However, the DP is complex and spans multiple sub-systems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). To ensure feasibility and manage risk, we must decompose the FR into sub-FRs and allocate each to the appropriate sub-module. The four sub-FRs identified in the refined_fr section (FR-NGHE-002.1 through FR-NGHE-002.4) provide a clear decomposition path. Additionally, the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate, but we must define concrete measurement and coordination mechanisms (e.g., Cycle Time Monitor, Latency Budget Interface) to make the DP realizable. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify these mechanisms and reduce implementation risk.
decision.justification_detail: The high safety criticality tailoring factor requires that we carefully validate that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety. This validation is best done at the sub-level, where we can analyze each sub-FR (vehicle timing, teleoperation latency, safety monitoring) in isolation and then verify their integration. Proceeding to ZIGZAG_DOWN allows us to address this concern systematically.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The teleoperation system shall introduce latency and control overhead not exceeding allocated cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: Safety services shall not introduce blocking delays that degrade cycle time below target, except during safety-critical interventions.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report cycle time metrics via telemetry for performance tracking.

assumptions: CT_target is achievable within vehicle mechanical limits and network latency constraints.
Local autonomy can be safely implemented for time-critical sub-cycles in Teleoperation.
Non-blocking safety monitoring can detect and respond to hazards within acceptable latency.
Teleoperation latency variability (jitter) is manageable via QoS enforcement on Network Link.
Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will expose cycle time metrics via standardized Telemetry interface.
Cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
open_risks: Risk: CT_target may be unrealistic; mitigation: validate target against vehicle mechanical limits and network latency constraints early in design.
Risk: Teleoperation latency variability may cause p95 to exceed p50 significantly; mitigation: design for worst-case latency, not average; implement adaptive control to handle jitter.
Risk: Safety Services non-blocking design may be difficult to achieve; mitigation: prototype safety monitoring architecture early; validate latency impact via testing.
Risk: Cycle time measurement may be inconsistent across sub-modules; mitigation: define standardized Cycle Time Metrics interface; validate measurement algorithm via field trials.
Risk: Latency budget enforcement may require real-time coordination; mitigation: implement Latency Budget Interface with low-latency query/response; validate responsiveness via testing.
evidence_needed: Vehicle mechanical timing data: load time, travel time, dump time under representative duty cycle.
Network latency characterization: round-trip latency distribution (p50, p95, p99) under site conditions.
Safety Services latency impact: worst-case delay introduced by safety checks; validation that delay is within cycle time budget.
Teleoperation control latency: operator command processing time, vehicle response time; validation that total latency is within budget.
Field trial data: measured p95 cycle time under representative duty cycle; comparison against CT_target; safety event correlation analysis.
